title: A vertical of Vega Sicilia: taking a deep dive into Spain’s most celebrated wine producer
summary: UK wine merchant Berry Bros & Rudd have just launched an offer of some back vintages of Vega Sicilia. (The
content:
UK wine merchant Berry Bros & Rudd have just launched an offer of some back vintages of Vega Sicilia. (The offer is here: https://www.bbr.com/mature-vega-sicilia-unico-valbuena)

So they invited some press and private customers for a rather grand tasting of these wines, which was hosted by winemaker Gonzalo Iturriaga.

Gonzalo started as technical director of Vega Sicilia 11 years ago. ‘There is something unique about Vega Sicilia,’ he says. And as for winemaking, ‘You don’t need any artifice – the terroir gives us everything.’

The key job, he says, is to find the al dente moment for harvest. And then when the grapes arrive in the winery, the approach taken is determined by the characteristics of the vintage. ‘Our objective is to be humble,’ he says.

Vega Sicilia age their wines for a long time. ‘The long ageing process is a unique way of making wines,’ says Gonzalo. To begin with, the wines start out in small oak, and both French and American oak are used. After a year, they decide where each barrel is destined. Some of the wines are less structured and will go to Valbuena, with another year in old oak. All the Merlot is used for Valbuena.

Unico spends 5.5 years in barrel, beginning with 18 months in small barrels, and then the rest of the time in 22 000 litre casks. This lengthy élevage takes the thick, structured young wines and refines them.

Vega Sicilia Valbuena 5° 2021 Ribera del Duero, Spain
Complex and bold with nice density. Grainy and spicy with good concentration, weight and structure. This is quite serious. 94/100

Vega Sicilia Valbuena 5° 2020 Ribera del Duero, Spain
Nice weight here with some elegance but also lots of structure. Nice intensity with cherry and berry fruits to the fore, and some grip. Very stylish. 95/100

Vega Sicilia Valbuena 5° 2018 Ribera del Duero, Spain
This is developing some appealing floral aromatics. Sweetly textured, and opening out nicely. There’s some grippiness, but also texture and purity, showing real elegance and some spicy oak on the finish. Everything is in harmony. 96/100

Vega Sicilia Valbuena 5° 2016 Ribera del Duero, Spain
Aromatic and fine; silky and spicy. Has resolved tannins with some warmth and spiciness. Sleek, warm berry fruits and a bit of grip on the finish. 95/100

Vega Sicilia Unico Reserva Especial NV Ribera del Duero, Spain
This 2026 release blends together 2011, 2012 and 2014. Smooth, long, elegant and refined with sweet cherry and plum fruit, some richness, and a slight saltiness on the finish. Such a wide dynamic range. A beautiful wine. 97/100

Vega Sicilia Unico Reserva Especial NV Ribera del Duero, Spain
This is the 2016 release, which blends together the 1996, 1998 and 2002 vintages. This shows beautiful evolution. Warm, spicy and herbal, with elegant red cherry and plum fruit. Lovely spicy framing. Refined and harmonious: this is still precise, even though it is showing plenty of development. 97/100

Vega Sicilia Unico 2016 Ribera del Duero, Spain
Lovely aromatics: warm and sweetly fruited, showing floral red cherry and black cherry fruit with a little development. Good intensity on the palate, which is ripe and rounded with lovely fruit and fine-grained tannins. Has a very sweet fruit profile. 95/100

Vega Sicilia Unico 2012 Ribera del Duero, Spain
Concentrated, saline and intense with appealing blackcurrant and black cherry fruit. Ripe, seamless and well integrated with concentrated, bold, slightly structured fruit. Real depth here. 96/100

Vega Sicilia Unico 2009 Ribera del Duero, Spain
This is concentrated, rich and refined with some sweet berry and blackcurrant fruit showing measured development. There’s a savoury, saline edge to the rich blackcurrant fruit on the palate. Warm and concentrated. 96/100

Vega Sicilia Unico 2006 Ribera del Duero, Spain
This is showing lots of development on the nose: herbs, spice and iodine, as well as sweet berry fruits. In the mouth it has lovely elegance. Spicy, with some firmness, but also warm, sweet fruit and a salty twist. Very stylish with nice intensity. 96/100

Vega Sicilia Unico 2005 Ribera del Duero, Spain
Warmly aromatic with herbs and a hint of turned earth, and notes of iodine and spice. The palate is warm, spicy and slightly earthy, with soft structure. Ripe, rich and mellow, this is a rich, warm style. 95/100

Vega Sicilia Unico 2004 Ribera del Duero, Spain
Complex, layered and elegant with lovely pure blackcurrant and black cherry fruit, with good depth of flavour. There’s some warm spiciness, too. Has richness, but also a linear, pure quality. 97/100

Vega Sicilia Unico 1996 Ribera del Duero, Spain
Nice maturity here. Warmly spiced berry fruits with richness and also complexity, and notes of herbs, iodine, salt. Black cherries and ripe berry fruits, with great concentration. Beautifully resolved and drinking perfectly now. 97/100

See also: https://wineanorak.com/2020/09/10/visiting-vega-sicilia-one-of-spains-most-revered-wineries/

title: Visiting Te Kano, an important producer with vineyards in Central Otago and Waitaki
summary: Website: https://www.tekanoestate.com/ It was in 2018 that I visited Te Kano for the first time. This relatively new producer had
content:
Website: https://www.tekanoestate.com/

It was in 2018 that I visited Te Kano for the first time. This relatively new producer had just bet big on a site in a subregion called Northburn, where they had just planted 32 hectares of vines, with plans to add 9 more. This overlooks Lake Dunstan, opposite Pisa. I revisited 7 years later to see how it was going.

This is a grand project. ‘We are on a 120 hectare site,’ explained winemaker Dave Sutton, ‘with 40 hectares of vineyard on it.’ There aren’t many vineyards on this side of Lake Dunstan at this end. ‘To the north of us is Cloudy Bay, and then it becomes Bendigo,’ says Dave.

What led them to make such an investment here? ‘The investment was there to be made; the land was available. It was large enough and diverse enough to plant a variety of clones and rootstocks, and varieties. We wanted to have a complete wine project, from whites through to reds. We needed a spectrum of sites within one site.’

Glaciation was important in forming this site. ‘You can see where glaciation has carved out the side of the mountain through successive periods.’

The bedrock is schist, but there are also other influences. ‘We have some tumbled glacial moraine outwash that’s overlaid with wind-blown loess, ground down rock dust that has been blown and sedimented. There are some silt deposits, and there are areas of pedogenic lime.’

‘It is like 17 vineyards stuck together.’

The wind has a profound influence. ‘It is complicating, and at times it is unbearable. It is the defining characteristic of this place.’

The site was planted in 2018 and the first wines came from 2020. So there are a few vintages now. What is the talent of this site? ‘There are a lot of exciting things. It forces you to have a close relationship with your vineyard team and the place. There is no way to broadscale farm in a homogenous way. You need to know every part. It is learning the characteristics of each of the sub-plots.’

Te Kano used ground penetrating radar to map the soil variation, and match variety and rootstock appropriately. Irrigation zones also match these various soil types.

Northburn is the main vineyard for Te Kano, but they also have three others. Two are in Bannockburn. The first is Jerome, which has incredibly sandy soils. The second is Eliza, opposite Mount Difficulty, off Felton Road, which has richer soils. Each of these vineyards are around 5 hectares.

But they are also present in Waitaki. They bought Pasquale, who had stopped producing. This was mainly for the winery (they were about to plan and build one at Northburn). Dave went to look at Pasquale to buy some equipment, and realized that this would be ideal (it was about to be bulldozed). So the Te Kano wines are all made there, and they have 11 hectares of vines.

We began tasting with a vertical of Northburn Chardonnay. This was planted in 2018, with a mix of clones. On the higher glacial terraces 648, Mendoza and 1066. The lower moraine, outwash gravel terraces, they have 95, clone 6 and 121. The single vineyard always comes from the higher glacial terrace. They struggled with getting enough shade on the fruit with the young vines, and the bony soils, which give whispy canopies. They’ve been working on more dappled light and less sun exposure, while still allowing air movement.

Te Kano Northburn Chardonnay 2021 Central Otago, New Zealand
Clone 1066. A cooler season with a significant wind event late in the season that caused some stress. Pressed to barrel with no settling, wild ferment. Linear and bright with taut citrus fruit and some peach and pear richness. There’s a hint of popcorn and toast, with a twist of pineapple and nice focused acidity, but a twist of bitterness too. Real vitality and focus here, offering a lot of flavour but also a sense of precision. 93/100

Te Kano Northburn Chardonnay 2022 Central Otago, New Zealand
Clone 1066. A warmer season, but it was quite wet. This is supple and linear with bright citrus fruit driving things, and then some apricot and pineapple exoticism. Nice freshness with a linear quality and some nice taut acidity in the mix. Very satisfying, tending to elegance. 94/100

Te Kano Northburn Chardonnay 2023 Central Otago, New Zealand
Mendoza from the top terrace and 95 from the alluvial gravels. A nice even season. La Niña year, with more northerly winds. Nice purity and weight here with bold peach, pear and pineapple fruit with some fine spicy notes. Lovely detail with a nice acid line and some spicy minerality, and even a hint of flint. 94/100

Next, a vertical of Estate Pinot Noir. This can vary in terms of it sources. Since 2022 there has always been an element of Waitaki in the blend. When they started working with Waitaki they used less whole bunch. The 2021 has 15% whole bunch, but since then none because Dave wanted to temper the inherent greenness of the Waitaki fruit.

The Felton Road vineyard is now organic. After conversion the vines looked stressed and there was more hardness to the tannin, but now it’s much more in balance. There has been a reduction of yield from 8 tons/ha to 6 tons/ha, but with less need for crop thinning.

Te Kano Estate Pinot Noir 2021 Otago, New Zealand
100% Felton Road fruit, 15% whole bunch. Lovely floral nose with sweet red cherries and plums. There’s a lovely silky quality to the texture with supple cherry fruit. Lots of fruit. This is balanced and very drinkable with lovely purity and a nice twist of green. This is really good. 94/100

Te Kano Estate Pinot Noir 2022 Otago, New Zealand
13% alcohol. The first year with some Waitaki (6%), and a different season as well with a rainfall into the harvest season. 42% Bannockburn and 52% Northburn. There was some whole bunch (13% of the batch was 50% whole bunch). Fresh and sappy with red cherry and redcurrant fruit, showing some crunch and brightness. There’s a touch of redcurrant here, too. 94/100

Te Kano Estate Pinot Noir 2023 Otago, New Zealand
13% alcohol. In 2023 they introduced a premium tier called Landwalker for Chardonnay and Pinot Noir, so some of the top lots went into that (but then 25% went back . More Waitaki (close to 15%). This is bright and fruity with some nice floral cherry notes, and also some bright berries and a bit of grippy structure. Nice intensity to this wine. 93/100

Te Kano Landwalker Pinot Noir 2023 Central Otago, New Zealand
This has almost 15% Waitaki, 42% Felton Road (Eliza) and the 43% Northburn. This is textured with some sour cherry and wild strawberry. This has real volume in the mouth but also good structure with a lovely concentration of fruit, with some fine spicy detail and lots of flesh. It’s delicious and plush but also repays some attention. 94/100

Then, a vertical of the Northburn Cabernet Franc. It is just Te Kano and Sato who make Cabernet Franc in Central Otago.

Te Kano Northburn Cabernet Franc 2021 Central Otago, New Zealand
First vintage. No added sulfites. Baked black cherry on the nose, with some savoury notes. The palate is textural and quite firm but has lovely black cherry and blackberry fruit with some gravelly structure. Lots of interest here with some silkiness, and no Cabernet Franc greenness. Lovely weight here. An experimental wine that sold out immediately at $55. 91/100

Te Kano Northburn Cabernet Franc 2022 Central Otago, New Zealand
Some sulfites this year, and no oak. This is bright and red fruited, with some nice green. Nervy and bright with lovely sappy red fruits. Juicy and expressive. Supple and expressive with real finesse. A lighter-style Cabernet Franc. 92/100

Te Kano Northburn Cabernet Franc 2023 Central Otago, New Zealand
Used barrels. Supple and bright with a good concentration of fleshy black cherry fruit with some blackcurrant. Nice weight of fruit with a bit of density and some structure. This is really nicely balanced with flesh and structure. 93/100

Te Kano Blanc de Noirs 2023 Central Otago, New Zealand
13% alcohol. Organic, all from Bannockburn. This is bright, lively and fresh with sweet citrus fruit and a bit of pear and cherry, with nice acidity. Pure and refreshing. Nice texture. 89/100

Te Kano Rosé 2022 Central Otago, New Zealand
This is nicely balanced with a core of strawberry and cherry fruit, rounded and expressive. Has a sweet core of fruit and finishes with fine spicy detail. 89/100

Te Kano Pinot Gris 2023 Central Otago, New Zealand
Lovely aromatics, orange peel, citrus and cherry. This has texture and depth with nice spicy detail and some grapey hints. Very satisfying and expressive. About 50% Waitaki. 92/100

Te Kano Waitaki Chardonnay 2022 Otago, New Zealand
This was the first vintage after dormancy. Clone 95. This has depth and energy with pear and peach and some bright citrus, with real stuffing: some spicy, savoury detail. Nice texture with some richness but also energy with nicely integrated oak. 94/100

Te Kano Northburn Chardonnay 2022 Central Otago, New Zealand
This is fresh and textural with pear and white peach, showing some fruit sweetness, a bit of softness of texture, with lovely purity and weight. There’s a little exoticism here with lovely balance. 94/100

Te Kano Bannockburn Chardonnay 2022 Central Otago, New Zealand
Finely spiced (Mendoza, 2008 plantings). This has freshness and texture with lovely pear and peach fruit with some brightness, and some good acidity. Fresh and expressive with lovely balance and a bit of energy. 93/100

Te Kano Life Chardonnay 2022 Central Otago, New Zealand
Lovely focus and balance here with a fine structure to the pear and citrus fruit with a hint of peachiness. Nice detail on the palate with good acidity. It’s a really beautifully balanced wine. 95/100

Te Kano Northburn Sauvignon Blanc 2022 Central Otago, New Zealand
Mostly barrel fermented, three clones (MS and two Loire clones). Beautiful aromatics with grapefruit and lemon as well as some smokiness. Grapefruit and elderflower on the palate. Very stylish with nice texture and depth. So stylish and multi-dimensional. 94/100

Te Kano Life Pinot Noir 2020 Central Otago, New Zealand
14% alcohol. Lovely cherry and raspberry fruit with a twist of sour cherry on the finish. Lovely balance with a nice savoury twist to the red fruits, with some noticeable structure supporting the sweet fruit. There’s some herb and pot pourri character, finishing crunchy. Lovely. 94/100

Te Kano Waitaki Pinot Noir 2022 Otago, New Zealand
Lovely fresh red cherry nose with some white pepper and floral berry fruits. Some rose petal. The palate has nice texture and some iodine notes, with silky cherry and plum fruit. Amazing elegance here, much more red fruited than black. On the cusp of herbal but still has sweet fruit. 95/100

Te Kano Bannockburn Pinot Noir 2021 Central Otago, New Zealand
Quite a bit of whole bunch here. This is ripe and textured with sweet berry and cherry fruit with fine spicy notes. Supple and expressive, and then finishes with some nice firm tannins. There’s a nice freshness on the finish. 94/100

Te Kano Northburn Pinot Noir 2022 Central Otago, New Zealand
Floral and sweetly fruited with soft strawberry and cherry fruit. Nice balance here with some red cherry on the palate. There’s a fine spicy edge to this wine. Very expressive and with some mid-palate softness, showing elegance. 95/100

Te Kano Riesling 2022 Otago, New Zealand
10% alcohol. Lots of Waitaki and a bit of Northburn. Lively and bright with some sweetness countering the high acidity. Juicy and vibrant with a lovely citrus core. 50 g/l of sugar, pH 2.9. Really balanced and fine. 92/100

title: Champagne Esterlin, an over-performing coop making superb wines
summary: Champagne Esterlin Website: https://www.champagne-esterlin.com/en/house/ Champagne Esterlin is a small co-operative based in Epernay. They began in 1948 when three families
content:
Champagne Esterlin

Website: https://www.champagne-esterlin.com/en/house/

Champagne Esterlin is a small co-operative based in Epernay. They began in 1948 when three families from Mancy (a village that’s part of the Coteaux Sud of Epernay) joined together to form a cooperartive, with 29 members and a vineyard area of 23 hectares. Now there are 400 members with 211 hectares of vines.

At Esterlin, they tend to use more age than normal for many of their wines. I tasted through the range, and was really impressed: there are some really good wines here.

The main winery is based in Mancy, and they also have two other pressing stations in the vineyards in the Sezannais and Marne Valley. They have a fancy house on the Avenue de Champagne in Epernay, too.

Champagne Esterlin Brut Éclat NV France
This was bottled in July 2019 and disgorged in May 2025 with a dosage of 7 g/litre. Alert, live and toasty on the nose with nice citrus brightness, a touch of pear and cherry, finishing bright and slightly saline. This is really good, with lovely focus and complexity. 92/100

Champagne Esterlin Rosé Éclat NV France
This was bottled in January 2022, disgorged June 2023 with a dosage of 7 g/litre. Bright and fruity, with pear, apple and cherry notes. Really lively and delicious with primary fruit and a touch of sweetness. Such juiciness here. Delicious stuff. 90/100

Champagne Esterlin Blanc de Blancs Éclat NV France
Bottled in January 2014, disgorged December 2018 with a dosage of 8 g/l. This is lively, toasty and really fine with lovely crystalline citrus fruit, some salinity, and fine spices. There’s a freshness here but also some richness, with this beautiful salty lick and a touch of sweetness from the dosage. This is excellent. 94/100

Champagne Esterlin Brut Nature 2009 France
Bottled May 2010, disgorged in March 2022 with no dosage. This is overtly toasty and lively with some lovely assertive lemony fruit, as well as a salty character. Very expressive and quite delicious: the lack of dosage reveals the lively, focused, toasty character of the wine so well. Impressive. 93/100

Champagne Esterlin Cléo Blanc de Blancs 2012 France
Bottled July 2013, disgorged March 2024, dosage 8 g/l. Base wine is barrel fermented. Lively, bright and linear with lovely citrus fruit, as well as a touch of green apple and fine toastiness. It’s complex, layered and quite salty on the finish with real depth and complexity. There’s a touch of herbal, spicy depth here, and it finishes bright and energetic. Lovely stuff. 95/100

title: The impressive wines of Wolfgang Seher, Weinviertel, Austria
summary: Website: https://www.weingutseher.at/ Based in Platt, in the Weinviertel region, Weingut Wolfgang Seher has 18 hectares of vines in two villages.
content:
Website: https://www.weingutseher.at/

Based in Platt, in the Weinviertel region, Weingut Wolfgang Seher has 18 hectares of vines in two villages. In Platt they have calcareous loess and clay, and then in Obermarkersdorf they have granitic soils some 12 km from the border. Wolgang took over running the Seher family winery in 2001. They are 70% Grüner Veltliner but also grow Riesling, Sauvignon Blanc, Pinot Blanc and Gelber Muskateller and then for reds Zweigelt, Pinot Noir, Cabernet Sauvignon and Merlot. On average they produce 60 000 bottles a year.

Wolfgang Seher Grüner Veltliner 2025 Weinviertel, Austria
This is fermented and matured in stainless steel. Fresh, dry and slightly peppery, with nice brightness and purity. Really lively and expressive: lovely. 91/100

Wolfgang Seher Platter Grüner Veltliner 2025 Weinviertel, Austria
From chalky loess soil. Matured in stainless steel. Bright, pure and linear with nice green apple and lemon fruit with a hint of pepper. Nice texture here: a stylish wine. 91/100

Wolfgang Seher Ried Nussberg Grüner Veltliner 2025 Weinviertel, Austria
On primary rocks. Stainless steel. Juicy and fresh with a nice peppery edge to the pure, linear fruit. Quite textured, but it stays bright, with nice energy. 92/100

Wolfgang Seher Ried Kapellenberg 1OTW Grüner Veltliner 2024 Weinviertel, Austria
This is matured in 500 litre barrels with some battonage, and then finishes off in stainless steel on the lees. Loess soils. Fine, long, peppery and elegant. This is really refined and textural with a bright edge and plenty of detail. 94/100

Wolfgang Seher Ried Feuerberg 1OTW Grüner Veltliner 2024 Weinviertel, Austria
Granite, on primary rocks. Fermented and matured in 500 litre barrels with some battonage, then finishes off in stainless steel on lees. Lovely freshness and brightness here with lots of detail. Linear citrus and pear fruit, as well as some citrus, with potential for further development. 94/100

Wolfgang Seher Neue Zeit Grüner Veltliner 2023 Weinviertel, Austria
This means number 8. It’s a selection of three single vineyards, and the wine was first made in 2012. It spends 2 years in large old oak. Lively, fine, textural and peppery. This is quite bright and has a nice future ahead of it. Lovely texture, and a long, mineral, saline finish. 95/100

Wolfgang Seher Wilde Rebe 2025 Weinviertel, Austria
This wine is made from grapes grown using a minimal pruning system. They start with a low canopy like Burgundy and then this grows to 1.5 m with minimal pruning. For the first few years yields are a little high, but then they settle down, and maturity is about 2 weeks later. This allows them to make fresh wines, with smaller berry size. 66% Grüner Veltliner, 34% Muskateller and Riesling. Alert fruity, peppery, grapey nose. Juicy and focused on the palate with lovely weight. Fruity and expressive with good acid, showing nice balance and personality. 93/100

Wolfgang Seher Urgenstein Riesling 2025 Weinviertel, Austria
Fruity, lively and bright. Lovely balance with crystalline fruit and good energy and acidity. Dry but textured, finishing salty. 93/100

Wolfgang Seher Weissburgunder Kalkmergel 2025 Weinviertel, Austria
50% stainless steel, 50% 500 litre barrels. Blended before bottling. Fruity and lively and bright with nice texture. Lovely focus here with good energy. Very stylish. 93/100

Wolfgang Seher Sauvignon Blanc Silt 2025 Weinviertel, Austria
Half steel half barrel. Subtle and very stylish with a mineral edge and nice green herby characters. Citrus and mineral, with real presence, showing lovely weight in the mouth. 93/100

Wolfgang Seher Rosé 2025 Weinviertel, Austria
11% alcohol. Zweigelt, Cabernet Sauvignon and Merlot. Direct pressed. Very palate in colour with some linear personality. Gastronomic with lovely cherry and citrus fruit. Textured and stylish. 91/100

Cask samples of reds:

Wolfgang Seher Roter Oktober 2023 Weinviertel, Austria
This blends Zweigelt with Merlot and Pinot Noir. Fresh with nice acidity. Linear a bright with appealing berry fruits. 90/100

Wolfgang Seher Merlot Tommergei 2023 Weinviertel, Austria
Fresh, linear and bright with a slight tarriness. Juicy, bright, pure. 91/100

Wolfgang Seher Pinot Noir Sandstein 2023 Weinviertel, Austria
Juicy and bright with nice energy and a bit of grip. Real focus here with lovely fruity intensity. 92/100

title: Hacienda el Ternero is making impressive wines from the north of Rioja
summary: Hacienda el Ternero is a little unusual in Rioja in that it’s a single estate, making wine from its own
content:
Hacienda el Ternero is a little unusual in Rioja in that it’s a single estate, making wine from its own vineyards. Most of the best known Rioja wineries don’t own grapes, but buy in from growers.

Ternero is a village in the north of Rioja, tucked into the foothills of the Obarenes Mountains, which are a continuation of Sierra Cantabria. This is the coldest part of Rioja, with poor rocky soils that have high limestone content. Altogether they have 125 hectares of vines, and they use a proportion of this to make 100 000 bottles a year, and they sell the rest of the grapes.

I’d not come across Ternero before, but these are really smart wines at sensible prices.

UK agent is Ellis of Richmond

Hacienda El Ternero La Pera Branco 2023 Rioja, Spain
Textured and rich with pear and peach. Stainless steel then concrete means that the fruit shows through. So textural with lovely purity and a touch of salinity. 92/100

Hacienda El Ternero Selección Especial Rioja Branco 2024 Spain
100% Viura, planted 1990, wild yeast, 500 litre French oak, no sulfites until bottling. This is concentrated, rich and mineral with lovely intensity. Lovely saline notes on the finish. Subtle oak adds some spiciness. Aniseed. Bold, ripe yellow fruits. 93/100

Hacienda El Ternero Torno Rioja Crianza 2021 Spain
14.5% alcohol. Younger vines. Shorter maceration. Used oak. Stylish and grainy and mineral with some crunchy red fruits. Supple and ripe with nice definition. This is really good: ripe but well defined. 93/100

Hacienda El Ternero Rioja Selección Especial 2018 Spain
14.5% alcohol. Rich, bold, spicy and woody, with nice depth and richness. Real weight here, but also freshness and chalky tannins. Nice intensity here, developing in a good direction. 92/100

Hacienda El Ternero Rioja Reserva 2016 Spain
14%. From two vineyards, one planted in 1965 and one at 550 m that’s younger. Old French oak. Mineral and stony. Ripe but restrained with a fine spicy edge to the cherry and raspberry fruit. Lovely. 94/100

title: Video: Spur pruning masterclass with Livio Tognon of Simonit & Sirch
summary: Pruning grape vines is a critical role in viticulture. Vines are perennial plants and almost always, they need to be
content:
Pruning grape vines is a critical role in viticulture. Vines are perennial plants and almost always, they need to be pruned in the dormant season. The decisions made at this stage not only affect the growth in the following year, but also affect the vine performance in years to come. Simonit & Sirch are a pruning consultancy who have worked hard to prune in the best way possible: their soft pruning approach is designed to help vines perform well over many decades.

Here, one of their consultants demonstrates the approach when it is applied to spur pruning.

title: Authentic Wines, a collaborative natural wine project from Portugal’s Lisboa region making wines under the Favonius label
summary: Website: https://authenticwines.pt/ Authentic Wines is the name of the winery, while Favonius is the name they give to the wines.
content:
Website: https://authenticwines.pt/

Authentic Wines is the name of the winery, while Favonius is the name they give to the wines. It’s a collaborative project between agronomist Pedro Cabanita, who is involved in the world of biostimulants, and winemaker Rodrigo Martins of Espera. The vineyards are in the Lisboa region, and winemaking is natural.

Favonius Arinto 2024 Lisboa, Portugal
This, like all the wines Favonius wines, is raised in stainless steel. Linear with crystalline citrus fruit, lovely complexity and intensity, and a salty finish. 93/100

Favonius Pet Nat 2025 Lisboa, Portugal
Touriga Nacional, disgorged. Bright with lovely purity and direct cherry fruit. Dry and detailed. 92/100

Favonius Curtimenta 2024 Lisboa, Portugal
Fernão Pires fermented on skins. Fresh, perfumed, lovely texture pure citrus with some peach and grapefruit peel. Delicious, balanced. 94/100

Favonius Tinto 2024 Lisboa, Portugal
Touriga Nacional. Spends just 3 days on skins. Lighter in colour and slightly faded. Ginger and orange peel nose. Detailed, spicy palate. Stewed strawberry finishing sour cherry. 92/100

title: Highlights: Château La Négly La Brise Marine Blanc 2021 La Clape, France
summary: Website: https://boutique.lanegly.com/en/ Nice to see this top Languedoc performing well with a bit of bottle age, and this back vintage
content:
Website: https://boutique.lanegly.com/en/

Nice to see this top Languedoc performing well with a bit of bottle age, and this back vintage is stocked in UK supermarket Tesco to boot. Current vintage is 2025 and has a blend of Bourboulenc, Grenache Blanc, Rolle and Roussanne. Retail in France is €12.90.

Château La Négly La Brise Marine Blanc 2021 La Clape, Languedoc, France
13.5% alcohol. Pleasantly surprised by this white blend of Roussanne, Bourboulenc and Clairette from La Clape in the Languedoc. It’s almost five years old, sealed with a synthetic (plastic) cork, but it’s really fresh and detailed. Harmonious citrus and pear fruit with some richer peachy notes, hemmed in by some nice spicy character and just a little structure. There’s a trace of saltiness on the finish, too. Lovely density and complexity here. 92/100 (£23 Tesco in the UK, also £18 at Quercus Wines)

title: Revisiting the brilliant low-intervention wines of Espera, from Portugal’s Lisboa region
summary: Website: https://www.esperawines.pt/en-gb Espera is a natural wine producer based in Alcobaça, in the Lisboa wine region of Portugal. Soils here are
content:
Website: https://www.esperawines.pt/en-gb

Espera is a natural wine producer based in Alcobaça, in the Lisboa wine region of Portugal. Soils here are clay limestone. Rodrigo Martins and Ana Leal farm five hectares of vines in Alcobaça and another five in nearby Óbidos. The climate here is moderated by proximity to the ocean. The wines are made in a low-intervention style and they are lovely.

Here’s a report from last year.

Espera Bical Arinto 2024 Lisboa, Portugal
Lovely freshness and tension allied to depth of flavour with a fine acid line and bold pear and peach fruit. Lovely. 93/100

Espera Curtimenta 2024 Lisboa, Portugal
12 days maceration, Fernão Pires. Crystalline and fine with a grapey edge to the sweet pear and citrus fruit. Lively and rich at the same time. 93/100

Espera Palheto 2024 Lisboa, Portugal
2 vineyards field blends of more than 20 varieties. Lighter red. Cherry fruit with some vivid citrus fruit showing nice weight. Warmly textured. 93/100

Espera Alvarinho Na Anfora 2022 Lisboa, Portugal
Beautifully floral and expressive with sweet peach and some table grape. The palate is grippy and grainy with spicy framing to the sweet fruit. So lovely. 94/100

Espera NatCool 2024 Lisboa, Portugal
Castelão. Younger vineyards, stainless steel. Beautiful raspberry and cherry fruit with nice purity and tartness. Very stylish and focused. 93/100

Espera Castelão 2023 Lisboa, Portugal
Structured and with nice acid showing bright raspberry fruit. Lovely weight and fruit intensity. 94/100

Espera Método Ancestral 2025 Lisboa, Portugal
Lovely fruit purity here strawberry and cherry with nice weight. Lovely. 91/100

title: Exploring speciality coffee and its parallels with fine wine: planning for the future, looking at new coffee varieties
summary: Just as grapevines are sensitive to the climate, coffee varieties only thrive within narrow climatic bounds. And with climate change,
content:
Just as grapevines are sensitive to the climate, coffee varieties only thrive within narrow climatic bounds. And with climate change, coffee production is at risk. For this reason, Mió are busy trialling new varieties of coffee.

99% of the world’s coffee comes from two species. There’s Coffea arabica (referred to simply as arabica) and Coffea canephora (referred to as robusta). Arabica is descended from the original coffee trees in Ethiopia, and represents 62% of plantings. It comes in a range of varieties (Mió have Red and Yellow Catucaí; Mundo Novo; Red, Yellow and Pink Bourbon; Acaiá; Arara; Red and Pink Gesha). Arabica makes finer, milder, more aromatic coffees and the beans fetch higher prices. Robusta, which is 38% of coffee plantings, gives strong, bold flavours with some bitter undertones. It mainly goes to blends and instant coffees. It’s widely grown in central and western Africa, Indonesia, Vietnam and Brazil. It can stand hotter, more humid climates and is more resistant to climate change. Robusta has a higher level of 2-methylisoborneal, giving typical earthy notes.

At Mió, one of the research projects they have initiated is looking more carefully at a genetic variant of canephora called conilon. This isn’t a separate variety, as such, but a genetic variant with slightly shorter stature and ability to ripen in cooler climates than regular robusta. Mió haven’t been growing canephora before, because it is too cold and too high altitude. ‘Ideally, canephora wants to be above 26 °C, says Ana, ‘and at an altitude of about 200 m. Here were are at about 800 m and it gets cold in winter.’

Mió have hired Dr Lucas Louzado who was formerly at the University of Epsírito Santo, a state in Brazil where a lot of canephora is grown. He has worked hard on improving the quality of conilon, bringing it into the speciality coffee realm. Louzada wanted to try canephora in Minas Gerais where Mió are based. Canephora might be a solution to climate change, as arabica suffers as the climate gets warmer. Mio don’t think this is the main solution, but they were willing to try it and test it. ‘We selected 150 different clones of canephora,’ says Ana. ‘Arabica is a self-pollinating tree so you can plant the same variety over one plot and it will grow well. With canephora, you need multiple types, because they are not self-pollinating. When you plant canephora you plant different clones of the same type. For Arabica you can plant the beans as seeds and they will grow into a plant. Canephora doesn’t grow like this: if you plant a seed, it will grow, but it will be a new clone that’s a blend of the two parents. If you want to maintain the same clone type across a plot, you need to use the plant itself. You bend them over and cut a piece off the bottom a side trunk, and then plant that.’ It will be interesting to see what results they get from these canephora plantings.

Mió are also experimenting with different arabica varieties, and we saw a plot where Lucas Louzada is assessing new varieties and even new species of coffee that could prove promising in the future. This is part of a large collaborative project, and they have planted lots of different varieties, managing the plants just the way they would manage their other plots. The partner scientists have more than 300 plots around Brazil where they are doing the same thing.

In the experimental plot, he showed us a new species that shows a lot of promise.

They have planted Coffea zanguebariae which they obtained from Mozambique. It is s indigenous to southern Tanzania, northern Zimbabwe and northern Mozambique including Ibo and Quirimba islands, where it grows in dry deciduous forests and riverine and coastal thickets.

It is resistant to heat and disease, and the flavour is completely different, he says. In two or three years it will be possible to make coffee from these plants. ‘The idea is to have more wild species in this plot to study, to see their adaptation, and to assess their sensory profile.’ Zanguebariae is completely new,’ he says.

SPECIALITY COFFEE AND ITS PARALLELS WITH WINE

  • A visit to Fazenda Mió in Brazil to catch the harvest
  • Regenerative farming in coffee production at the Sombra agroforestry project at Fazenda Mió in Brazil
  • Planning for the future: looking at new coffee varieties
  • Sensory assessment of coffee, with parallels to wine tasting: cupping, grading and describing, with Dr Fabiana Carvalho
